findings.description: The entire Typography Hierarchy table (font per level/channel: Agenda for print headlines, Nunito Sans for digital/email, Aptos fallback, plus available weights and the campaign-headline exception for live Agenda text) is not represented by either candidate rule. This is a major, distinct governance area (font family selection by channel/level) that must be its own rule(s), not omitted.

payload.description: Use Agenda for print headlines, Nunito Sans (bolder weights, Title Case) for digital/email headlines with live-text exception limited to campaign headline, Nunito Sans for body/footers, Aptos as Word/PPT/Excel fallback; restrict weights to documented available weight sets per font.

findings.description: The [GENERAL] email font-stack (CSS fallback) rule (u0010) is a distinct governance fact about CSS font-family stacking and webfont loading behavior, separate from the pixel/line-height type scale. It appears to have been dropped or improperly folded into rule_lisraya_email_type_scale; it should be split into its own rule.

payload.description: Email CSS font stack must be 'Nunito Sans', Verdana, Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif, loaded via @font-face/Google Fonts where supported, to guarantee fallback in Outlook and webfont-stripping clients.

findings.description: The general visual-hierarchy principle ('use a clear type scale so reader knows what to read first/next/last; never use two adjacent text blocks at same size+weight to signal different hierarchy levels') is a distinct, brand-wide typography/hierarchy constraint not tied specifically to email pixel scale, but it has no corresponding rule in the candidate set.

payload.description: Never use two adjacent text blocks at the same size and weight to signal different hierarchy levels; hierarchy must be visually distinguishable via type scale.

findings.description: Unit u0018 ('Never set running text below 12px') carries an explicit governance exception: 'ISI sizing is owned by the locked component.' This scope carve-out (exemption for locked-component-owned content) is not reflected as a facet/exception on rule_lisraya_email_type_scale, risking incorrect universal application.

payload.exception: ISI sizing is owned by the locked component and is exempt from the 12px minimum running-text rule.

findings.description: This rule merges two distinct thresholds: a universal minimum of 150% line-height for all body copy, and a stricter [GENERAL] conditional minimum of 160% specifically for light text on dark backgrounds. The conditional (scope-dependent) sub-constraint is not distinguished, so a consumer cannot tell that dark-background contexts require a higher threshold than the base rule.

payload.description: For light text on dark backgrounds, line-height must be increased to at least 160% (e.g., 16px/26px), exceeding the general 150% minimum.

findings.description: Unit u0016 imposes a usage-scope restriction on secondary/supporting body text (14px/21px): 'use sparingly, never for primary content.' This qualitative usage governance (frequency/priority restriction) is not captured as a distinct facet on the clustered rule, which currently only encodes numeric sizing.

payload.usage_restriction: Secondary/supporting body text (14px/21px) must be used sparingly and never for primary content.

findings.description: The 16px body-copy minimum is described in the source as a brand-wide mandate ('brand-mandated minimum for body copy') rather than an email-only constraint, yet it is clustered solely under rule_lisraya_email_type_scale (scoped to email type scale). Scope should be broadened or a cross-reference added so the constraint is not misread as email-exclusive.

payload.scope_note: 16px minimum for body copy is a brand-wide mandate, not limited to email context; email type scale table is one application of it.
